perturbation.original_text: This Agreement allows us to disclose Confidential Information (defined below) to each other, to our own affiliates and to the other’s affiliates, under the following terms. An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement allows us to disclose Sensitive Data (defined below) to each other, to our own affiliates and to the other’s affiliates, under the following terms. An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us.
perturbation.explanation: Changing “Confidential Information” to “Sensitive Data” without defining "Sensitive Data" introduces ambiguity. Later sections define “Confidential Information,” creating a contradiction as to which definition applies, the original or the new one.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a.What is included. "Confidential Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: A. Are designated in writing as “confidential” at the time of their disclosure and include : (1) corporate financial records or corporate proprietary information (e.g. trade secrets) exempted under the California Public Records Act, California Government Code Sections 6250, et seq .; or (2) information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party. B. The County’s sensitive security information, or technical data, programs, software (including configuration or source codes ), technical information, screen shots, customer information, employee records, computer network, architectural or engineering information, exploitable data, information protected by privacy law, or other information that is treated as confidential by the County, or is prohibited from being disclosed for any reason pursuant to law, statute, regulation, ordinance, or contract .
perturbation.justification: The original text defines and uses "Confidential Information." The changed text replaces this with "Sensitive Data," which is not defined. The contradicted text defines "Confidential Information." By replacing "Confidential Information" with "Sensitive Data," the contract becomes unclear as to whether the definition in the contradicted text still applies, creating a contradiction. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology' as it leads to in-text contradictions because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: "Confidential Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: A. Are designated in writing as “confidential” at the time of their disclosure and include : (1) corporate financial records or corporate proprietary information (e.g. trade secrets) exempted under the California Public Records Act, California Government Code Sections 6250, et seq .; or (2) information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: "Protected Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: A. Are designated in writing as “confidential” at the time of their disclosure and include : (1) corporate financial records or corporate proprietary information (e.g. trade secrets) exempted under the California Public Records Act, California Government Code Sections 6250, et seq .; or (2) information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Confidential Information" to "Protected Information" within the definition section, while still referring to “confidential” designation creates internal inconsistency. The text defines "Protected Information" using criteria that refer to "confidential" designation, even though the main term has changed. This creates ambiguity about what actually constitutes Protected Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a.What is included. "Protected Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: A. Are designated in writing as “confidential” at the time of their disclosure and include : (1) corporate financial records or corporate proprietary information (e.g. trade secrets) exempted under the California Public Records Act, California Government Code Sections 6250, et seq .; or (2) information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Confidential Information" to "Protected Information" introduces a strong in-text contradiction. The modified text redefines "Protected Information" but then refers to information being designated as "confidential," which is not aligned with the new term. This contradiction is evident in the contradicted text, which is the definition of Protected Information and still relies on the designation of "confidential". The explanation highlights that this misalignment creates ambiguity about what actually constitutes Protected Information, thus making the text contradict itself.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Sensitive Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Sensitive Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Here, 'Confidential Information' has been changed to 'Sensitive Information' within the phrase, but further in the sentance it is still being referred as Confidential Information. This creates an uncertainty, about whether both terms are the same or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Sensitive Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Sensitive Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement. Neither Party is required to restrict work assignments of Representatives who have had access to Confidential Information. Each Party acknowledges that they cannot control the incoming information the other Party will disclose in the course of the business relationship between the Parties , or what each Party’s Representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. Each Party agrees that use of information in its Representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of the Parties’ respective products or services does not create liability for that Party under this Agreement or trade secret law. Both Parties mutually agree to limit what each Party discloses to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers consistently to 'Confidential Information'. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' at the beginning of the sentence but retains 'Confidential Information' later in the same sentence and in the contradicted text. This creates a contradiction because it introduces uncertainty about whether 'Sensitive Information' and 'Confidential Information' are the same or different, making the obligations related to each unclear. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition, as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq .
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Private Data if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq .
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Confidential Information" to "Private Data" while referencing the California Public Records Act, which deals with "Confidential Information", introduces conflict. It becomes unclear if "Private Data" is subject to the same disclosure rules under the Act, because the Act specifies handling of 'Confidential Information' requests, not explicitly 'Private Data'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Private Data if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy. If, in the absence of a protective order, the recipient determines that it is required to disclose such information, it may disclose without breach of this Agreement only Confidential Inform ation specifically required and only to the extent compelled to do so . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Private Data' while still referencing the California Public Records Act (CPRA). The contradicted text explicitly outlines procedures for handling requests for 'Confidential Information' under the CPRA, including notice to allow for objections. By changing the initial term to 'Private Data', it's unclear if the CPRA procedures apply to 'Private Data', creating a conflict because the contradicted text assumes 'Confidential Information' is governed by the CPRA and the changed text does not make it clear whether 'Private Data' is.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement has been negotiated and executed in the State of California and shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of California, without reference to conflicts of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement has been negotiated and executed in the State of California and shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of Nevada, without reference to conflicts of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada creates a direct contradiction with the references to California law earlier in the document (e.g., California Public Records Act). The contract now claims to be governed by Nevada law but relies on California statutes, causing significant legal uncertainty. This change leads to legal ambiguity regarding which laws govern the agreement, especially considering earlier clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy. If, in the absence of a protective order, the recipient determines that it is required to disclose such information, it may disclose without breach of this Agreement only Confidential Inform ation specifically required and only to the extent compelled to do so . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Nevada. The contradicted text explicitly references and relies upon the 'California Public Records Act' and 'California Government Code section 6250 et seq'. By changing the governing law, the agreement now operates under Nevada law while still referencing specific California statutes. This creates a direct conflict, because the California Public Records Act would not apply if the governing law is Nevada. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.
